myths.Agent_1:
Long ago, before the sky had color, the world was an endless canvas of gray. The Creator, tired of the monotony, tasked two celestial siblings, Lumis and Umbra, to paint the heavens. Lumis, the elder sister, carried a heavy bucket of liquid gold, while Umbra, the younger brother, held a delicate jar of deep, velvet ink.

Lumis eagerly began to splash her gold across the expanse, creating the brilliant, blinding light of day. She was so captivated by the warmth that she refused to stop, leaving Umbra waiting in the margins. The earth below began to scorch, the oceans boiling under an endless, unforgiving sun.

Seeing the world suffering, Umbra took matters into his own hands. He hurled his velvet ink across the sky, completely washing away the gold. The cooling darkness brought immediate relief to the earth, but also a chilling, quiet freeze. The siblings began to fight, wrestling for control of the sky, until the Creator finally intervened.

He decreed they must share the canvas. Lumis would paint the day, bringing life and warmth, and Umbra would paint the night, bringing rest and peace. Where their paints met at dawn and dusk, the sky bruised into beautiful shades of twilight, a daily reminder of their eternal pact.
myths.Agent_2:
Long before mortal man walked the earth, the vast sky was ruled by two great beasts: Ignis, the Sun Bird, whose feathers were woven from pure dawn, and Volos, the Storm Wolf, whose roar was the thunder. They despised each other. Ignis loved the quiet warmth of the day, while Volos reveled in the chaotic chill of the dark.

One twilight, as the boundaries of their realms briefly overlapped, Volos ambushed Ignis. The massive wolf lunged, jaws snapping shut on the bird’s radiant tail. Ignis shrieked, a sound that cracked the very heavens, and beat his wings fiercely. As they collided, the pure heat of the Sun Bird met the icy friction of the Storm Wolf’s heavy coat. 

From this violent, desperate clash, a tiny, glowing seed fell to the earth below. It landed in a bed of dry pine needles. The seed hissed, then bloomed into a dancing, hungry golden entity. It was the first Fire. 

Terrified by this strange new magic that belonged to neither of them, the beasts separated forever. Now, mankind eternally tends to the fallen seed, a beautiful remnant of a celestial war, feeding the hungry child of storm and sun.

myths.Agent_2: Myth: Before time flowed, the world was a still, gray rock, overseen by two great beast-spirits: Ignis the Sun Bird and Volos the Frost Wolf. They were brothers, tasked by the Creator to breathe life into the barren earth. Ignis swept over the land, his golden feathers shedding warmth. Forests sprouted, and rivers thawed. But Ignis was zealous; his relentless heat began to scorch the young forests into deserts. Seeing the destruction, Volos intervened. He howled, summoning thick, velvet storm clouds to blot out the sun. His icy breath cooled the burning earth, bringing a deep, restful freeze. Yet, under Volos's unbroken darkness, the world began to wither in the cold. The brothers attacked one another, heat and frost clashing in a tempest of thunder and light. From this celestial war, a spark of balance fell to earth—the first Fire—giving mortals a way to survive the cold. The Creator, awakened by the noise, separated the beasts. He decreed they must chase each other in an eternal circle. When Ignis rules, it is summer; when Volos catches him, winter falls. And where they briefly meet, the world experiences the gentle transitions of spring and autumn.
myths.Agent_1:

Lumis, resentful of her restricted hours, would linger at the edge of dusk, letting her golden brush drag across the horizon. Umbra, eager to bring the cooling peace of night, would rush his arrival, his dark ink splashing violently against her fading light. 

During one particularly fierce twilight, Umbra’s impatience caused his jar of ink to collide directly with Lumis’s golden bucket. The impact shattered a fragment of Lumis’s vessel, sending thousands of tiny, glowing droplets scattering deep into Umbra’s dark canvas. 

Umbra tried furiously to wash them away with his deepest shadows, but the drops had already set into the velvet fabric of the night. Lumis laughed, realizing that even in the depths of her brother’s domain, her glorious light would forever remain. 

These scattered droplets became the stars—a permanent, glittering reminder of their celestial rivalry. To this day, humanity looks up at the night sky, finding comfort in the fractured light of Lumis that guides them safely through Umbra's darkest hours.

Myth: The creation of the stars did little to quell the rivalry between Lumis and Umbra. While Lumis boasted of her golden sun and the sparkling droplets trapped in her brother's sky, Umbra grew weary of his dark domain being defined only by her fractured light. He desired a masterpiece of his own, something to command the velvet night and rival her blinding day. 

For ages, Umbra scoured the cosmos, gathering the largest shattered remnants of Lumis’s golden bucket that had scattered during their great clash. He plunged these fragments deep into the frozen, quiet waters at the edge of the world, cooling the furious gold until it turned into a smooth, brilliant silver. 

With great effort, Umbra hoisted this massive silver pearl into his dark canvas. It did not burn with the harsh, scorching heat of Lumis, but instead glowed with a cool, gentle radiance. He called it the Moon. 

Lumis was envious of its quiet beauty, realizing her brother had transformed her broken vessel into a beacon of peace. Now, the Moon stands as Umbra’s proudest creation, a silent guardian that watches over the sleeping earth, pulling the tides and calming the restless seas.

Whenever her grip was particularly fierce during an eclipse, tiny fragments of the silver pearl would break off. Ignited by Lumis’s lingering heat and cast adrift in Umbra’s vast, dark ocean, these shattered pieces of moonlight became too heavy for the sky to hold. They plummeted toward the earth below, streaking across the velvet canvas in brilliant flashes of silver and gold.

Umbra wept for his damaged masterpiece, sweeping his shadows across the sky to catch the falling pieces, but they were always too fast. Lumis, however, reveled in the bright scars they left behind in her brother's domain.

Mortals below look up in wonder at these sudden streaks of light, calling them shooting stars. They close their eyes and whisper their deepest wishes to the falling fragments, unaware that they are wishing upon the cosmic debris of an endless sibling rivalry, the beautiful casualties of a celestial war between day and night.
